title: Plate and Sheet: Rolling Process and Laminar Discontinuities
narrationScript: Take everything we learned about ingots and squeeze it flat. That is the story of plate and sheet. The slab that enters a rolling mill carries the ingot's leftovers. Pipe that was not fully cropped, blowholes, clusters of inclusions, and segregated centerline metal. Rolling does not remove those things. It flattens them, stretches them in the rolling direction, and lays them parallel to the plate surfaces. By the time the slab has become a twenty five millimeter plate, a fist sized cavity has become a thin, wide lamination, and a string of inclusions has become a stringer. Knowing that one fact tells you almost everything about how to test plate. Walk through the process first. The route starts with a slab, either rolled down from an ingot or cast directly on a continuous caster. The slab is reheated to around twelve hundred degrees Celsius and driven back and forth through powered rolls, first in a roughing stand, then in finishing stands, getting longer and thinner with each pass. Plate is the heavy flat product, generally about a quarter inch and up. Sheet is thinner, and is often cold rolled afterward for finish and dimensional control. Through the whole journey the reduction happens in thickness, so any internal feature gets squeezed flat and elongated in the direction of rolling. Now the discontinuities. Lamination is the headline. It comes from unhealed pipe, from blowholes whose walls were oxidized, from heavy inclusion clusters, and from centerline segregation. It is planar, it lies parallel to the rolled surfaces, it sits most often at or near mid thickness, and it can be tight enough that the two faces touch. Tight does not mean welded. Even a closed lamination keeps an oxide film and a whisper of air gap, and at a steel to air interface essentially all of the sound reflects. Stringers are the smaller cousins, elongated inclusions strung out in the rolling direction. At the surface you can find seams, slivers, rolled in scale, and in sheet, blisters where shallow gas pockets expanded during reheating. Here is the good news. A discontinuity lying parallel to the surface is the perfect target for a straight beam. Couple a longitudinal wave probe to the plate and the beam hits the lamination dead square. On the screen you see the lamination echo at its depth, usually near half thickness, and the back wall echo drops or vanishes because the lamination shadows it. If the lamination is bigger than the beam, the back reflection disappears completely and the lamination echo produces its own train of multiples at half the normal spacing. In thin plate the individual echoes crowd together, so we lean on the multiple echo pattern instead. A sound region rings with a long, evenly spaced, smoothly decaying train of back echoes. A laminated region cuts that spacing roughly in half and kills the train early. For near surface resolution, a dual element probe gets you close to the surface without drowning in the dead zone, and production mills run the same logic at speed in immersion, painting a map of every plate. One more reason plate testing matters. Angle beam weld inspection assumes the beam bounces cleanly between the plate surfaces. A lamination in the scanning zone hijacks the vee path, reflects the beam where you do not expect it, and falsifies every skip calculation you make. That is why weld procedures have you clear the scanning zone with a straight beam before any angle beam work. Finally, the standards. Plate specifications define the scanning pattern, the sensitivity, and the acceptance criteria. A common approach scans along grid lines on roughly nine inch centers, which is about two hundred twenty five millimeters, or in continuous parallel paths, with sensitivity set to a clear first back reflection in sound material. The classic acceptance logic reads like this. An area where the back reflection is completely lost, and which cannot be contained inside a circle of a specified diameter, often three inches or seventy five millimeters, is rejectable. Tighter quality levels shrink the circle, add one hundred percent coverage, and add edge zone scanning for plate that will be beveled and welded. When you find a lamination, you map it. Move the probe until the lamination echo drops by half or the back wall recovers, mark the boundary, and report the size, depth, and location the way the specification asks. Flat product, flat flaws, straight beam. Keep that picture and plate evaluation becomes a methodical, almost relaxing job.
imagePrompt: A technician kneels on a large steel plate in a fabrication shop, sliding a dual-element ultrasonic probe along a chalked grid line while a flaw detector beside him displays an A-scan trace.

sections.heading: The Rolling Process: Flattening the Ingot's Legacy
sections.body:
Plate and sheet begin as **slabs** — rolled down from ingots or cast directly on a continuous caster. The slab is reheated to roughly 1,200 °C and driven back and forth through powered rolls: a **roughing stand** takes the big bites, then **finishing stands** set thickness, profile, and surface. **Plate** is the heavy flat product (commonly about 6 mm / 1/4 in. and up); **sheet** is thinner and often **cold-rolled** afterward for gauge control and finish.

Rolling reduces thickness while width changes comparatively little, so the deformation stretches the metal in the **rolling direction**. Every internal feature inherited from the casting is squeezed flat and drawn out lengthwise:

- Unhealed **pipe** and oxidized **blowholes** become thin, wide **laminations** parallel to the surfaces.
- **Inclusion clusters** smear into **stringers** aligned with the rolling direction.
- **Centerline segregation** becomes a weak, dirty mid-thickness band — prime lamination territory.

This is why a plate's discontinuity population is so well organized: planar, parallel to the rolled surfaces, concentrated at or near **mid-thickness**, and elongated along the rolling direction. The same geometry that created these discontinuities is what makes them easy targets for a straight beam.

sections.caption: Rolling squeezes slab-stage pipe, blowholes, and inclusion clusters into thin laminations and stringers lying parallel to the plate surfaces.

sections.heading: Laminations, Stringers, and Surface Discontinuities
sections.body:
**Laminations** are the defining plate discontinuity: thin planar separations parallel to the surface, born from pipe, blowholes, inclusion concentrations, or segregated centerline metal. They range from open splits visible at a cut edge to **tight laminations** whose faces touch. Tight does not mean bonded: an oxide film and a microscopic air gap remain, and a steel-to-air interface reflects essentially **all** of the incident sound — which is why a lamination you cannot see at the plate edge still lights up the screen.

**Stringers** are elongated nonmetallic inclusions — individually small, often in mid-thickness colonies. They degrade through-thickness properties (the lamellar-tearing problem under welded restraint) and they pepper the screen with small echoes during scanning.

At and near the surface live the rolling defects: **seams** (rolled-out surface cracks or ingot surface defects), **slivers** (partially attached flakes of metal), **rolled-in scale**, and — in sheet — **blisters**, near-surface gas pockets that expanded on reheating. Plate **edges** deserve special attention: laminations frequently run out at sheared or flame-cut edges, exactly where a fabricator will later cut a weld bevel.

Orientation summary: virtually everything that matters in flat product is **planar and parallel to the rolled surfaces** — the one orientation a straight beam cannot miss.

sections.title: Tight laminations still reflect
sections.body: The faces of a tight lamination touch, but an oxide film and a trace of trapped air separate them acoustically. The steel-to-air impedance mismatch reflects essentially **100% of the energy**, so echo amplitude does not distinguish tight from open. Judge a lamination by its **mapped extent**, not its brightness.

sections.heading: Straight-Beam Response: Echo, Shadow, and Multiples
sections.body:
Couple a longitudinal-wave probe to plate and a lamination presents itself broadside. The signature has three parts:

- **A discrete echo** at the lamination depth — most often near **T/2**.
- **Loss of back reflection**: the lamination shadows the back wall. When the lamination is larger than the beam, the back wall vanishes completely.
- **Halved multiples**: in 50 mm plate, a mid-thickness lamination puts echoes at 25, 50, 75 mm and beyond, while sound plate puts back-wall multiples at 50, 100, 150 mm. Half the spacing is the give-away.

Because most laminations are larger than the beam, size them by **boundary mapping** rather than amplitude: move the probe until the lamination echo falls by **6 dB** (half amplitude) or the back wall recovers, mark that point, and repeat around the perimeter to trace the laminar area. Record the depth from the echo position and reference the outline to the plate edges and rolling direction.

Keep beam coverage honest. Index spacing must overlap the effective beam width, and sensitivity is set on **sound material** — typically adjusting the first back reflection to a specified screen height — then left alone while you scan.

sections.caption: A mid-thickness lamination returns its own echo and multiples while shadowing the back wall; in thin plate the halved multiple-echo spacing is the give-away.

sections.heading: Thin Plate, Near-Surface Zones, and the Angle-Beam Trap
sections.body:
In **thin plate** the lamination echo and back wall crowd too close to resolve, so use the **multiple-echo pattern**: a sound region rings with a long, evenly spaced, smoothly decaying train of back echoes, while a laminated region halves the spacing and chokes the train early. A **dual-element** probe handles **near-surface** laminations hiding in a single-element probe's dead zone, and a **delay line** does similar work on thin material.

Production mills automate the same logic: **immersion** or water-column squirter systems scan full plates at speed, often in **through-transmission**, where a lamination announces itself as a drop in transmitted energy, and results plot as a **C-scan** map. The acceptance logic is identical to manual work.

Now the trap. Angle-beam weld examination assumes the beam bounces cleanly between plate surfaces. A lamination in the scanning zone intercepts the V-path, reflects the beam early, and falsifies every skip-distance and depth calculation — it can hide a real weld flaw or conjure a phantom one. That is why weld procedures require a **straight-beam check of the base-material scanning zone** before any angle-beam work, and why your plate lamination report matters to the welding inspector downstream.

sections.heading: Codes, Grids, and Acceptance
sections.body:
Plate UT runs on a few well-worn specification patterns — think of the ASTM A435 / A577 / A578 family and the EN 10160 quality classes, invoked by the purchase order:

- **Coverage**: scanning along **grid lines on roughly 225 mm (9 in.) centers**, in continuous parallel paths, or 100% for tighter classes; **edge zones** often get mandatory 100% coverage where weld bevels will be cut.
- **Sensitivity**: set to place a clear first back reflection from sound material at a specified screen height, maintained unchanged during scanning.
- **Evaluation trigger**: any indication causing **complete loss of back reflection**, or echoes exceeding a stated fraction of it.
- **Acceptance logic**: the classic clause rejects a laminar area whose total loss of back reflection **cannot be contained within a circle of specified diameter — commonly 75 mm (3 in.)**. Tighter classes shrink the circle or limit how many such areas may exist per plate or per unit area.

Map every reportable area by the 6 dB-drop boundary or back-wall recovery, then sketch its size, depth, and position. Angle-beam supplements (the A577 pattern) target what a straight beam under-calls — inclined discontinuities near sheared edges, for example. Evaluate to the document that governs the order, not to habit.

sections.caption: Grid scanning on roughly 225 mm centers with two mapped laminar areas — one containable within the 75 mm acceptance circle, one rejectable.

figures.title: Rolling turns casting flaws into laminations
Grid scanning and the containment-circle rule

interactive.title: Grid Scan: Map the Lamination
interactive.intro: You are examining 25 mm carbon-steel plate to a straight-beam plate specification. The procedure calls for scanning along grid lines on 225 mm centers with a 5 MHz dual-element probe, sensitivity set so the first back reflection from sound material reaches full screen height. Acceptance: any area of complete back-reflection loss that cannot be contained within a 75 mm diameter circle is rejectable.

steps.prompt: Mid-way along a grid line, the back-wall echo collapses to zero and a new echo stands at about half the back-wall screen position. What is the most likely cause?
options: Couplant starvation under the probe
A mid-thickness lamination shadowing the back wall
Far-surface roughness scattering the beam
A spurious mode-converted wedge echo
steps.feedback: An echo near half thickness plus complete loss of back reflection is the textbook lamination signature. Couplant loss would kill the whole screen, including the new echo.

steps.prompt: What does the specification expect you to do next?
options: Reject the plate immediately and stop scanning
Scan the surrounding area in a continuous pattern and map the boundary of complete back-reflection loss
Switch to a 70-degree shear-wave probe to size the depth
Raise the gain until the back wall returns, then continue
steps.feedback: Grid findings trigger continuous scanning around the indication. Map the boundary with the 6 dB-drop or back-wall-recovery method before making any disposition.

steps.prompt: You map complete back-reflection loss over an area about 60 mm by 40 mm. Disposition under the stated criterion?
options: Rejectable — any complete loss of back reflection is cause for rejection
Acceptable — the area can be contained within a 75 mm diameter circle, so record it and move on
Acceptable only after weld repair of the lamination
Rejectable — laminations are never acceptable in rolled plate
steps.feedback: The diagonal of a 60 by 40 mm area is about 72 mm, so a 75 mm circle contains it: acceptable under this criterion, but it must still be recorded with size, depth, and location.

steps.prompt: Near one plate edge that will be beveled for a butt weld, you find continuous back-reflection loss along 300 mm of the grid line. Best call?
options: Accept — laminar indications near edges are cosmetic
Record it and reject or refer it — the area exceeds the containment circle and sits in a future weld zone
Accept it if the lamination echo stays below 50% screen height
Have the edge ground smooth and retest after welding
steps.feedback: A 300 mm laminar area cannot fit any 75 mm circle, and edge zones feeding weld bevels are exactly where laminations cause fit-up, lamellar-tearing, and angle-beam problems. Document it and disposition per the specification.

checkQuestions.question: Why do laminations in rolled plate lie parallel to the surfaces, usually near mid-thickness?
options: Because rolling pressure pushes all discontinuities toward the surfaces
Because rolling flattens and elongates centerline pipe, blowholes, and inclusion clusters in the plane of the plate
Because laminations form during cooling after rolling, where stresses are planar
Because the roughing stand folds the slab surface over itself at mid-thickness
checkQuestions.explanation: Laminations are ingot-stage discontinuities — pipe, blowholes, inclusion clusters, centerline segregation — squeezed flat and stretched in the rolling plane; the originals sat near the slab centerline, so the laminations sit near mid-thickness.

checkQuestions.question: Using the multiple-echo method on 12 mm plate, a sound region shows back echoes spaced 12 mm apart. Over one zone the spacing drops to 6 mm and the train decays quickly. This indicates:
options: A lamination near mid-thickness (about 6 mm deep)
Increased plate thickness in that zone
A near-surface blister within the dead zone
Normal grain-noise variation in rolled plate
checkQuestions.explanation: A mid-thickness lamination halves the echo path, so the multiple spacing halves from 12 mm to 6 mm, and the extra reflections drain energy so the train decays faster.

checkQuestions.question: A lamination is so tight its faces are in physical contact, yet it still returns nearly all the beam energy. Why?
options: Contact pressure increases the acoustic impedance of the interface
An oxide film and microscopic air gap remain, and a steel-to-air interface reflects essentially 100% of the sound
Tight laminations resonate at the probe frequency
The faces cold-weld during rolling, creating a harder reflective layer
checkQuestions.explanation: Even a closed lamination keeps an oxide film and a trace of air between its faces; the enormous steel-air impedance mismatch reflects practically all incident energy, so tightness does not reduce the echo.

checkQuestions.question: A lamination is suspected just below the scanning surface, inside a single-element contact probe's dead zone. The best tool from this lesson is:
options: A higher-gain setting on the same probe
A dual-element probe (or delay line) for near-surface resolution
A 45-degree shear-wave wedge
A lower-frequency single-element probe
checkQuestions.explanation: Separating transmit and receive elements (or adding a delay line) moves the dead zone out of the part, restoring resolution just below the surface where single-element probes are blind.

checkQuestions.question: Before angle-beam examination of a plate butt weld, the base material beside the weld is checked with a straight beam because:
options: Codes require measuring plate thickness before any weld examination
A lamination in the scanning zone reflects the angled beam early and falsifies skip-distance and depth calculations
Straight beams calibrate the shear-wave velocity used later
Laminations amplify the angle-beam signal and cause overcalls only
checkQuestions.explanation: Angle-beam geometry assumes clean V-path bounces between plate surfaces; a lamination intercepts the path, so indications plot in the wrong place — it can hide real flaws or create phantom ones.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §2.2 (plate and sheet: rolling, discontinuities, response, codes/standards)
Straight-beam and angle-beam plate practices such as ASTM A435, A577, A578 and EN 10160 (cited generically)
